questions.question: What are access modifiers in TypeScript classes?

choices.a: `public`, `private`, `protected` — they control property visibility and are enforced at runtime
choices.b: `public`, `private`, `protected` — compile-time visibility modifiers erased in the JS output
choices.c: Only `private` and `public` exist; `protected` is not part of TypeScript
choices.d: Access modifiers are the same as JavaScript's `#private` field syntax

questions.explanation:
TypeScript adds THREE access modifiers:
• `public` (default): accessible everywhere
• `private`: accessible only within the declaring class (TypeScript enforcement only)
• `protected`: accessible within the declaring class and subclasses

CRITICAL: They are COMPILE-TIME ONLY. The emitted JavaScript does NOT enforce them — any code that accesses a `private` field at runtime through type casting can do so.

For TRUE private fields that enforce at runtime, use JavaScript's `#fieldName` syntax (ES2022 private fields). TypeScript supports these too.

```typescript
class A {
  #realPrivate = 1;  // JS private — truly inaccessible at runtime
  private tsPrivate = 2; // TS only — erased, accessible at runtime
}
```

questions.question: What is an abstract class and when should you use it over an interface?

choices.a: Abstract classes can be instantiated directly; interfaces cannot
choices.b: Abstract classes cannot be instantiated, may have concrete implementations for some methods, and produce actual JS (unlike interfaces which are erased)
choices.c: Abstract classes are used only when you need multiple inheritance
choices.d: Abstract classes and interfaces are completely interchangeable

questions.explanation:
An ABSTRACT CLASS:
• Cannot be instantiated directly (`new AbstractClass()` → error)
• Can have CONCRETE (implemented) methods, abstract (unimplemented) methods, and constructor logic
• IS emitted as a JavaScript class at runtime (unlike interfaces)

```typescript
abstract class Animal {
  abstract makeSound(): string; // must implement in subclass
  
  move(): void { console.log('Moving'); } // concrete — shared impl
}
```

Use ABSTRACT CLASS when:
• You have shared IMPLEMENTATION to provide to subclasses
• You have a constructor or state that subclasses inherit
• You want to enforce a CONTRACT that includes implementation

Use INTERFACE when:
• You only need a CONTRACT (no implementation)
• You want a class to satisfy multiple contracts (a class can implement many interfaces but only extend one abstract class)

questions.question: What is parameter property shorthand in TypeScript classes?

choices.a: A way to destructure constructor parameters into the class scope
choices.b: Prefixing a constructor parameter with an access modifier creates a class property and assigns it automatically
choices.c: It is only valid for optional constructor parameters
choices.d: It is a JavaScript native feature, not TypeScript-specific

questions.question: What is the `override` keyword in TypeScript 4.3+?

choices.a: It replaces the parent class method at the prototype level
choices.b: It explicitly marks that a method in a subclass is overriding a parent method — TypeScript errors if no such method exists in the parent
choices.c: It is required for all method overrides in TypeScript
choices.d: `override` is only used with abstract classes

questions.question: What is a mixin in TypeScript and what problem does it solve?

choices.a: A way to implement multiple inheritance through a composition of classes using functions
choices.b: A special syntax for merging two interfaces
choices.c: A runtime polyfill for missing class features
choices.d: Mixins are a banned pattern in TypeScript strict mode

questions.question: How does `implements` differ from `extends` in class declarations?

choices.a: `implements` copies properties into the class; `extends` inherits them
choices.b: `implements` checks at compile time that the class satisfies an interface/type contract without inheriting anything; `extends` inherits implementation
choices.c: A class can use `implements` with multiple classes

questions.question: What are TypeScript decorators and what is `emitDecoratorMetadata`?

choices.a: Decorators are pure type-level constructs erased at compile time
choices.b: Decorators are functions that modify classes/methods/properties at runtime; `emitDecoratorMetadata` emits `Reflect.metadata` calls for DI frameworks
choices.c: Decorators are only stable in TypeScript 5+ experimentalDecorators mode
choices.d: `emitDecoratorMetadata` is deprecated and removed in TypeScript 5
